coredata.dc:description: Removal of organics from wastewater is a tedious process and industries are looking for a facile, energy efficient strategy of wastewater treatment for producing clean water and recycling. Membrane technology is one of the efficient processes for wastewater treatment due to its energy efficient nature, simple operation and excellent performance. But, proneness of membrane fouling often limits its path to triumph. In this work, we have designed a ‘carbon dot/NiFe-layered double hydroxide (CD/NiFe–LDH)’ nanocomposite via in situ approach, which is coated on a poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) and cellulose based superhydrophillic membrane. The membrane is found to have excellent separation efficiency. The photocatalytic activity of CD/NiFe–LDH shows high oxidative degradation of the accumulated organics contaminants on the membrane surface providing self-cleaning ability and hence enhances the membrane’s lifetime. The membrane exhibits stable performance for 30 days continuous operation under UV-A light. The membrane flux is found to be ∼42 Lm−2 h−1 and 48 Lm−2 h−1 for crude oil–water emulsion and phenol respectively while rejection around ≥99 % is obtained for the both. The flux recovery ratio (FRR) of the post photo irradiated membrane is found to be 88.07 %. The membrane also shows organic solvent resistance property and prevents bacterial pathogens in wastewater.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Removal of organics from wastewater is a tedious process and industries are looking for a facile, energy efficient strategy of wastewater treatment for producing clean water and recycling. Membrane technology is one of the efficient processes for wastewater treatment due to its energy efficient nature, simple operation and excellent performance. But, proneness of membrane fouling often limits its path to triumph. In this work, we have designed a ‘carbon dot/NiFe-layered double hydroxide (CD/NiFe–LDH)’ nanocomposite via in situ approach, which is coated on a poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) and cellulose based superhydrophillic membrane. The membrane is found to have excellent separation efficiency. The photocatalytic activity of CD/NiFe–LDH shows high oxidative degradation of the accumulated organics contaminants on the membrane surface providing self-cleaning ability and hence enhances the membrane’s lifetime. The membrane exhibits stable performance for 30 days continuous operation under UV-A light. The membrane flux is found to be ∼42 Lm−2 h−1 and 48 Lm−2 h−1 for crude oil–water emulsion and phenol respectively while rejection around ≥99 % is obtained for the both. The flux recovery ratio (FRR) of the post photo irradiated membrane is found to be 88.07 %. The membrane also shows organic solvent resistance property and prevents bacterial pathogens in wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid industrial growth with simultaneous generation of hazardous waste is a major concern to the ecosystem. Petroleum industries have been generating huge amount of wastewater which contain crude oil in emulsified form along with other water-soluble organic contaminants [1–4]. Organic contaminants have carcinogenic as well as genotoxic effect on living organisms [5]. Phenol is one such organic contaminant which is abundantly found in petroleum industry wastewater and it is reported that, in river water phenol concentration is in the range of 40–100 mg/L [6]. When dispose in water stream, aquatic animals present in the waterbody consume these organic contaminants and transfer it to the food chain. Disposal of oily organic containing wastewater in soil effects its fertility. Therefore, removal and degradation of these contaminants is of vital importance. Various approaches have been done to remove these oily organic contaminants from wastewater which includes chemical as well as physical treatments. However, these conventional methods always fail in terms of the stability, rejection as well as durability of the separating media [7]. Membrane separation have been identified as the most trusted and advantageous separation process and have always been given the highest priority due to its advantageous nature like energy efficient separation with high selectivity, easy scaling up for larger volume separation and possible hybridization with other methods [8–11]. However, due to deposition of contaminants or separated targets, cake layer formation on membrane surface often produces fouling which results in flux declination, selectivity loss and short lifespan. Recovery of such fouled membranes requires repeated washing for prolonged use, which necessitates a lot of energy [12]. Thus, design of membrane with inherent fouling resistance property is highly needed. By including hydrophilicity and charge on the membrane surface, there have been considerable advancements achieved in the literature to lessen membrane fouling [13]. Nevertheless, cake layers continue to develop on the membrane surface over time. “Self-cleaning” membranes bring tremendous opportunities for applying in wastewater treatments which is devoid of additional cleaning procedure [14–16]. Self-cleaning membranes can be of mainly two types, electrocatalytic and photocatalytic. Comparatively, photocatalytic self-cleaning membranes are advantageous and more eco-friendly which only need access to ambient light source [17,18]. Research has been done on designing membrane for oil–water separation [19], desalination [20] including self-cleaning polymeric membranes integrating with photocatalyst [21]. Lv et al. have designed a photocatalytic membrane by impregnating layer of β-FeOOH nanorods over a layer consist of polydopamine and polyethyleneimine for dye separation and degradation [22]. Another photocatalytic self-cleaning membrane was developed by Liu et al. depositing g-C3N4@TiO2 heterojunctions over graphene oxide (GO) nanosheet [23]. Dong et al. designed a double defence system on a poly (vinylidene fluoride) derived membrane for minimizing the fouling [24]. Advanced oxidation process (AOP) is one of the most well-known and effective technique used for degradation of organic pollutants by means of photocatalyst utilization. Photodegradation of organic contaminants by designing efficient catalyst such as TiO2 based photocatalysts [25,26], UiO-66-NH2 frameworks [27], metal–organic-frameworks of mixed Ti–Zr [28], Fe2O3–TiO2–clay heterostructures [29], layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [30–32] and carbon-based nanomaterials [33,34] are most common. LDHs are established as one of the most effective and reliable high performance photocatalysts. Although LDHs contain metal/transition metals in its structure, their leaching probability is almost negative due to the stable arrangements of metals within the LDH structure. Due to its layered structure, LDH integration in membrane can provide high surface area for organics adsorption as well as a sieve-like channel for size exclusion of big organic molecules present in wastewater [35]. Even though an array of work has been reported on LDH-based photocatalytic membranes for pollutant photodegradation, a deeper understanding of how to modify LDH properties to increase membrane separation performance as well as photocatalytic degradation ability is still needed to be achieved in order to produce photocatalytic membranes at a commercial level. The usage of carbon dots (CD) as catalysts or co-catalysts in photocatalysis applications hasalso increased in an exponential manner because of their capacity to absorb sunlight in a broad range from UV to IR [36]. CDs have a flexible composition and surface functionalities that can give interaction opportunities withother materials which can improve interlayer charge separation [36,37]. Considering the properties such as effective surface redox reactions, rapid charge carrier migration, and excellent solar spectrum use, CD-based compounds can be applied as an appealing option for photocatalysis application [38,39].

To achieve an excellent photocatalyst, two key criteria must be addressed: enhancing the separation of electron-hole pairs upon light irradiation and inducing a red shift in the absorption spectrum to cover a broad range of wavelengths [40]. Single-semiconductor photocatalysts often fall short in meeting both requirements simultaneously, necessitating the use of heterojunction complexes composed of two semiconductors. Heterojunctions are formed through the intimate contact of different semiconductors. Traditional heterojunction types, such as Type-I and Type-II, exhibit strong photocatalytic activity but may sometimes struggle with efficient carrier separation [41]. The Z-scheme heterojunction presents an alternative with a similar energy band arrangement to the Type-II heterojunction but features a distinct electron transport pathway [42]. The exceptional migration pathway of Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts effectively addresses the limitations of Type-II heterojunctions [43]. The combination of two semiconductors, such as LDHs and CDs, holds promise for creating an effective Z-scheme heterojunction with high redox capacity.

The current study leverages the synergistic advantages of LDHs and CDs for the development of a chitosan-derived amine-containing CD-decorated NiFe–LDH nanocomposite as a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst via an in-situ approach. This method of in-situ synthesis of CD/NiFe–LDHs is novel, offering an energy-efficient process that eliminates the need for external energies such as ultrasonication or hydrothermal treatment. The synthesized CD/NiFe–LDHs have been incorporated onto a superhydrophilic membrane surface derived from waste materials, facilitating the selective removal of oily organics from industrial wastewater. Amine-containing CDs enable the chemical binding of the synthesized CD/NiFe–LDHs to the hydrophilic membrane using glutaraldehyde. This coating imparts an extremely hydrophilic surface, enhancing high flux and antifouling properties. The incorporation of amine-containing CDs is anticipated to provide high catalytic activity due to the presence of nitrogen atom [44]. The study demonstrates that CD/NiFe–LDHs act as efficient photocatalyst, conferring self-cleaning properties to the membrane by photocatalytic degradation of the rejected contaminants under UV-A irradiation on the membrane surface. The two primary precursors for the synthesis of the support membrane are waste PET bottles and waste cellulosic paper. The recycling of waste plastics to design organic solvent-resistant membranes is a novel approach, considering that PET is stable against most organic solvents [45,46]. To our knowledge, the combination of waste PET and photocatalysts for designing a photocatalytic self-cleaning membrane has not been previously reported. In contrast to conventional single-strategy approaches for achieving fouling-free membranes, this study employs a multifunctional membrane that exhibits high performance, self-cleaning properties, antimicrobial activity, and resistance to organic solvents.
Chitosan is used to synthesize carbon dot by following top-down approach [47]. The detail preparation procedure has been illustrated in Supplementary Materials (SM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ synthesis of CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite was done for the first time using in-situ co-precipitation method. Initially, nickel acetate tetrahydrate (0.15 mol) and ferric nitrate nonahydrate (0.05 mol) were added to 100 mL of water. The mixture was stirred using a magnetic stirrer for 15 min at 600 rpm and room temperature to prepare a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, 10 mL of CD solution was added to the solution and stirred for an additional 20 min. This combined solution was then added dropwise to an aqueous solution containing 150 mL of sodium hydroxide (0.1 mol) and sodium carbonate (0.2 mol) under constant stirring at 600 rpm and room temperature. The resulting dark yellow precipitate was recovered by centrifugation and washed three to four times with deionized water. The washed product was dried at 60 °C in a hot air oven for 12 h and stored for further use. For comparison studies, pristine NiFe–LDH was synthesized using a simple co-precipitation technique, maintaining a Ni to Fe ratio of 3:1 (Ni:Fe) [48].

The PET-cellulose nanofiber membrane used as the support membrane was developed according to our recently published methodology [49]. This preparation method involves the in-situ formation of cellulose nanofiber (CNF) in trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) solvent, to which polyethylene glycol-400 (PEG-400) and dry PET raw materials are added in specific amounts. The solution is stirred using a magnetic stirrer continuously for 3 h at room temperature. In the reaction mixture, the concentrations of PET, PEG, and CNF are maintained at 10 %, 12 %, and 2 % (w/v), respectively. Utilizing the produced dope solution, the target membrane is fabricated using the non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS) method (Fig. 1 ). The resulting membrane (PCM) is washed with distilled water and air-dried for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CD/NiFe–LDH coated PET-CNF nanocomposite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Using the crosslinking agent glutaraldehyde, the CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite is grafted onto the PET-CNF support membrane. Glutaraldehyde was selected as the optimal cross-linking agent based on the functional groups present in the CD/NiFe–LDH and the PET-CNF membrane (PCM) [49,50]. A 100 mL aqueous solution of CD/NiFe–LDH at a concentration of 0.6 % (w/v) was prepared. A square piece (10 cm × 10 cm) of the PET-CNF support membrane was used for the nanocomposite coating (Fig. 1). The coating process involved two steps: (i) first, the membrane was dipped into 30 mL of 25 % (w/v) glutaraldehyde solution for 5 min, followed by air drying; (ii) second, the glutaraldehyde-treated membrane was dipped into 60 mL of the CD/NiFe–LDH aqueous solution (0.6 % (w/v)). After 20 min of immersion, the membrane was removed and air-dried. This coating procedure was repeated one, two, and three times to vary the CD/NiFe–LDH content on the membrane surface, resulting in the membranes PCMLC1, PCMLC2, and PCMLC3, respectively. The compositions of the prepared membranes are provided in Table 1 . Additionally, it was observed that with increase in the number of coatings of the CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite on the membrane, the thickness of the membranes also increases (Table 1).

The separation of crude oil-based organic contaminants and phenol in the feed solutions is conducted using a cross-flow membrane permeation setup, schematically depicted in Fig. 2 . The membrane is fitted between two cylindrical compartments, supported by a macroporous nylon substrate. The compartments are fabricated using pressure-resistant polyacrylates. As illustrated in Fig. 2, a high-pressure resistant glass is affixed to the end of the upper compartment, allowing access for the UV-A light source to the membrane.

In the experimental setup, a circular membrane of area 14.5 cm2 is fitted into the membrane cell. Two types of feed solutions are prepared: a crude-oil water emulsion (FS1) and a phenol feed (FS2) as described in the Supplementary Material (SM). The FS1 and FS2 are circulated through the membrane using a peristaltic pump in continuous mode. Required pressure was applied using N2 gas for permeation. The permeate passing through the membrane is collected at regular interval of time. The samples are analysed by UV–vis spectrophotometer using calibration curve. The presence of oil droplets in both the feed and permeate is observed using an optical microscope. The oil and grease content in water is measured according to ASTM method 5520A [51]. Detailed procedures for this method are provided in the SM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CD, NiFe–LDH and CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The structural properties of the synthesized CD/NiFe–LDH have been elucidated by various characterization techniques and are compared with pristine CD and NiFe–LDH. XRD patterns obtained for CD, NiFe LDH and CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite have been illustrated in Fig. S1 of SM. Solid state UV analysis of CD/NiFe–LDH have been shown in Fig. S1. The band gap of the synthesized CD/NiFe–LDH nanocompsite is found be 2.32 eV. FTIR, TGA and SEM analysis of the synthesized nanomaterials along with the detail discussion have been provided in Fig. S1 of SM. Fig. 3 (A1 and A2) provides the TEM and HRTEM image of the synthesized CD, which reveals the spherical dot structure of CDs. Lattice spacing of 0.39 nm could be indexed as (002) crystallographic plane. Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) imaging of CD also confirms its crystalline nature and the presence of (002) plane in CD (Fig. 3(A3)). Similarly, Fig. 3(B1) and (B2) respectively shows the TEM and HRTEM images obtained for the synthesized NiFe–LDHs. Respective planes present in NiFe–LDH are identified with SAED imaging (Fig. 3(B3)) which also verifies its polycrystalline nature [46]. Lattice spacing of 0.24 nm is indexed as (015) crystallographic plane. TEM and HRTEM images of the in situ synthesized CD/NiFe–LDH have been shown in Fig. 3(C1) and (C2) respectively. Presence of two types of planes with lattice spacing 0.39 nm and 0.24 nm respectively can be indexed as the (002) and (015). SAED imaging in Fig. 3(C3) also verifies the presence of these planes. It can be revealed that lamellar morphology of the LDHs is beneficial for CDs to be assembled on the surface of the LDHs with strong electrostatic bonding interactions between them [52]. Moreover, Fig. 3(D1, D2 and D3) respectively shows the EDX mapping of the elements (C, N, O) present in the CDs. EDX mapping of CD/NiFe–LDH clearly confirms the presence of the constituent elements (C, N, O, Fe and Ni) (Fig. 3(E1, E2, E3, E4 and E5)). The presence of constituent elements is further confirmed via XPS analysis (Fig. S2). For NiFe–LDH, respective peaks of C1s, O1s, Fe2p and Ni2p are observed while in case of CD/NiFe–LDH, C1s, O1s, Fe2p, Ni2p and an additional N1s peak has been seen [53]. This confirms the successful formation of CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite. The dynamic light scattering method for size analysis is done to ensure the formation of CDs (Fig. 4 A). This fact is further supported by the fluorescence spectra of CDs (Fig. 4B), which clearly shows the photoluminescence property of the produced CDs.

From BET nitrogen adsorption desorption isotherm of NiFe–LDH and CD/NiFe–LDH, their mesoporous structure is confirmed (Fig. S3(A and B) in the SM). The surface area of NiFe–LDH has been increased from 71.064 m2/g to 96.350 m2/g in CD/NiFe–LDH which is confirmed by BET analysis (Fig. S3 (C) in SM). Consequently, it has been established that CD-mediated exfoliation of NiFe–LDH stacked layers reveals a greater surface area for possible utilization.

The XPS survey spectra of PCM and PCMLC2 membranes are shown in Fig. 5 A and Fig. 4B. In case of PCM membrane, only C1s and O1s peaks are observed while additional peaks of N1s, Ni2p and Fe2p along with C1s and O1s are observed in XPS survey spectra of PCMLC2 membrane which proves the successful incorporation of CD/NiFe–LDH. High scan deconvolution peaks of the corresponding elements are also shown in Fig. 5A and 5B. The presence of different functional group in the prepared membranes is verified by means of FTIR analysis and the obtained spectra are shown in Fig. 5C. In all three membranes, peaks around 3400 cm−1 stands for –OH and –NH stretching while peak around 2950 cm−1 stands for C–H stretching. In case of PCML and PCMLC2, the Metal-OH peaks observed in the range from 730 cm−1 to 490 cm−1 indicates the successful incorporation of NiFe–LDH in the membrane, while in PCM membrane, major peaks in this range are absent [48].

While considering the treatment of wastewater, hydrophilic membranes are always preferred which not only increase the flux but also minimize membrane fouling. Water contact angle analysis has been done for all the fabricated membranes. From the analysis of water contact angle of the prepared membranes in Fig. S4(A) of SM, it is observed that at zero second, the angle obtained for PCMLC2 membrane is lowest i.e. 230 which is almost absorbed in 60 s. Among PCMLC1, PCMLC2 and PCMLC3 membranes, PCMLC2 membrane has high water absorbing ability. The results of the membrane's water absorption percentage analysis and porosity measurements as shown in Fig. S4(B) and S4(C), respectively, are also comparable with this observation. The PCMLC2 membranes are found to have the highest percentages of both porosity (%) and water absorption (%).

The surface morphology and cross-sectional views of the prepared membranes are obtained by SEM analysis (Fig. 6 ). The SEM image of PCM membrane shows that it has a porous structure on its surface as well as throughout the cross section (Fig. 6(A1 and A2)). Nonhomogeneous coating of NiFe–LDH in PCML membrane is seen in Fig. 6(B1 and B2) which may be due to the lack of strong chemical crosslinking between membrane and NiFe–LDH. However, a homogeneous coating of CD/NiFe– LDH over the membrane PCMLC1, PCMLC2 and PCMLC3 is observed due to strong chemical interaction between the –NH2 and –OH groups of CDs with the –OH groups of the membrane. Fig. 6(C1, D1 and E1) indicated the change in surface morphologies of PCMLC1, PLMLC2 and PCMLC3 membranes respectively. With increase in number of coatings, the thickness of the coated layer increases as usual and is confirmed from cross-sectional images of membranes shown in Fig. 6(C2, D2 and E2). The pore size distribution of the membranes (Fig. S5) showed that the pore radius slightly decreases in the order PCM > PCML > PCMLC2. This is due to the coating of CD/NiFe–LDH on membrane surface, which reduces the average pore radius. The decrease in pore size of the membrane is also related to the results obtained from the SEM images where PCM membrane shows visible pores which disappeared in the coated membranes. The mesoporous structure of the membranes is established via BET nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. S6). Although the pore size of the membranes is found to decrease after coating, however, surface area of them is enhanced in the order PCM < PCML < PCMLC2 due to the greater surface area of CD/NiFe–LDH.

For a smooth surface with water contact angle θ < 90°, the hydrophilicity of the membrane increases with increasing surface roughness. Similarly, for a smooth surface with water contact angle greater than 90°, its hydrophobic character increases with increasing surface roughness [54]. Moreover, membrane with high surface roughness exhibits high efficiency towards removal of organic contaminants than smooth surfaces [55]. Surface roughness parameter (area roughness Sa) of the prepared membranes PCM and PCMLC2 are analysed by atomic force microscopy (AFM) and shown in Fig. 6(F) and 6(G) respectively. The area roughness (Sa) of the membrane PCMLC2 is found to be 674 nm, while in case of PCM the area roughness is 249.464 nm. The increase in roughness value can be explained from the impregnation of CD/NiFe–LDH in PCMLC2 membrane. The rough surface of PCMLC2 membrane will also offer large effective surface area of interaction with the target molecules than that in PCM membrane having relatively smoother surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal and mechanical property analyses of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Thermal and mechanical properties of a membrane determine its potential application in industrial sectors. From thermogravimetric analysis of the pristine membrane (PCM), mass loss from 484.1 °C to 560.7 °C has been observed for PET degradation and in the range of 303.2–416.5 °C, a slight degradation is observed which can be explained for the degradation of cellulose present in the membrane (Fig. 6H). In PCMLC2 membrane, mass loss at two stages is observed; the first one in between 176.3 °C and 337.5 °C corresponds to the degradation of CD/NiFe–LDH and other one in the range of 383.7–530.4 °C stands for cellulose-PET degradation (Fig. 6H). Mechanical strength of all the prepared membranes is analysed and shown in Fig. 6I. The ultimate stress of the membranes is increased in the order of PCM < PCML < PCMLC1 < PCMLC2 < PCMLC3. The bare PCM membrane possesses the lowest tensile strength of 0.85 MPa while PCMLC3 membrane possesses highest tensile strength of 2.75 MPa.

The performance of the membranes is studied using feed solution FS1 and FS2 in a cross-flow permeation set up (Fig. 2). Feed solution ‘FS1′ contains oily organics in water and ‘FS2′ contains phenol in water which are prepared in our laboratory. The permeation experiment of both feed solutions is done with varying pressures, flow rates and feed concentration for PCMLC2 membrane. All experiments are conducted at room temperature. The permeates obtained from each permeation experiments are analysed by UV spectrophotometer (Fig. S7). Effect of all the variables or factors are discussed in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of CD/NiFe–LDH concentration on membrane performance
ce:section.ce:para: The flux vs time graph of all the membranes for FS1 and FS2 are shown in Fig. 7 (A1) and (A2) where the pressure is kept constant at 1.5 bar and flow rate at 850 mL/min. Both graphs make it clear that the membrane flux decreases over time. Among all the membranes, PCMLC2 exhibits highest flux of 38 Lm−2 h−1 and 40 Lm−2 h−1 for FS1 and FS2 respectively (at pressure = 1.5 bar, flow rate = 850 mL/min). PCM itself shows good permeability due to its hydrophilic nature, and the hydrophilicity of CD/NiFe–LDH further enhances it. The modification of PCM with NiFe–LDH and CD/NiFe–LDH integrates higher flux efficiency. This is obvious from the enhancement of flux values in the order PCM < PCML < PCMLC1 < PCMLC2. In case of PCMLC3 membrane, the flux is higher than PCM, PCML and PCMLC1 membranes while using FS1 and relatively lower than PCMLC2. But while using FS2, the PCMLC3 membrane shows lower flux than PCMLC1 and PCMLC2 membranes. This can be explained from the increasing thickness of thin layer on the base membrane in PCMLC3 with increased amount of CD/NiFe–LDH. Interestingly, the PCMLC3 membrane is less hydrophilic in comparison to PCMLC2 membrane which can be confirmed from contact angle analysis (Fig. S4). This may be due to the agglomeration of the nanoparticles over the membrane after the optimal concentration [56]. Thus, from the performance analysis of all the designed membranes, PCMLC2 is established as the best performing membrane. Hence, we used this membrane for detail permeation study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of pressure, flow rate, feed concentration and temperature on membrane performance
ce:section.ce:para: The membrane permeation experiments are conducted under varying conditions of pressure, flow rate, and feed concentration using the PCMLC2 membrane. Fig. 7(B1) and (B2) display the flux versus time graphs at different pressures (1, 1.5, and 2 bar) for FS1 and FS2, respectively, with a constant flow rate of 850 mL/min. The highest fluxes recorded are 42 Lm−2 h−1 for FS1 and 48 Lm−2 h−1 for FS2 at 2 bar pressure. It is evident from Fig. 7(B1) and (B2) that flux decreases gradually over time as pressure increases. The volume of permeate passing per unit time and unit area increases with higher transmembrane pressure. The membrane's selective layer and porosity contribute to the rejection of targeted components. For PCMLC2 membranes at 1 bar pressure, the highest rejection of 99 % for FS1 and 99.1 % for FS2 were achieved. However, with increasing pressure, a slight decrease in rejection is observed as shown in Fig. 7(E1) and (E2). At higher pressure, some targeted molecules may penetrate the membrane pores, resulting in reduced overall rejection [57].

The flux of the PCMLC2 membrane is also measured at varying flow rate (650, 850, and 1050 mL/min) with a constant pressure of 1.5 bar, as shown in Fig. 7(C1) and 7(C2) for FS1 and FS2, respectively. The highest membrane flux recorded at the highest flow rate (1050 mL/min) are observed to be 39 and 42 Lm−2 h−1 for FS1 and FS2, respectively. The rejection study of the PCMLC2 membrane at different flow rate shows a slight decrease in rejection for both FS1 and FS2 (Fig. 7(F1) and (F2)). At higher flow rate, the membrane comes in contact with a higher volume of feed per unit time compared to lower flow rate. Due to the reduced contact time, some targeted molecules may be retained in the retentate before coming into close proximity with the membrane, explaining the decrease in rejection with increased flow rate.

Additionally, the permeation experiments were performed with varying concentrations of phenol for FS2, while the concentration of the crude oil-based feed (FS1) was kept constant. At the lowest concentration of FS2 (50 ppm), both flux and rejection of the PCMLC2 membrane are higher than those for FS1 at a higher concentration (100 ppm) (Fig. 7(D1) and (G1)). At higher concentration, the rate of phenol accumulation at the membrane's adsorption sites increases, leading to a decline in both flux and rejection [57].

Membrane permeation experiment for PCMLC2 is conducted by varying the temperature from 25 °C to 50 °C at flow rate 850 mL/min and pressure 1.5 bar. The results have been provided in Figure S15 in the SM. It is observed that with increasing temperature there is a slight increase in the membrane flux. On the other hand, with increasing temperature a gradual decrease in % rejection is observed. This may be attributed to the fact that, on increase in temperature there is swelling of the membrane structure including the pores. [58]. This ultimately results in increase in the mass flow of the solute through the membrane. Therefore, increase in temperature results in increasing flux of the membrane while % rejection decreases.

The microscopic images of oil droplets in the feed solution (FS1) and the permeate of FS1 are presented in Fig. S8. In the feed solution, oil droplets are observed at the microscopic level, whereas no oil droplets are detected in the permeate. Additionally, the yellowish color observed in the FS1 feed solution is completely absent in the permeate following treatment with the PCMLC2 membrane (Fig. S8). This confirms the effective removal of oil from the feed solution.

We have measured the flux of the membranes under various operating conditions, including pressure, flow rate, and feed concentration. These experiments were conducted to optimize the suitable operating conditions. From this, we have determined that a pressure of 1.5 bar and a flow rate of 850 mL/min were the optimal conditions. The long-term permeation analysis for the membranes have been discussed in Section 3.7.

The designed membrane PCMLC2 demonstrated appreciable flux and rejection under various operating conditions. To understand the outcomes and underlying causes, it is crucial to investigate the associated mechanisms. We identified three primary mechanisms that are directly related to the functionality of the PCMLC2 membrane: size exclusion, adsorption, and photocatalytic degradation as depicted in Fig. 8 A–C.

The targeted molecules in the feed solution with size larger than the membrane pores are rejected through a size exclusion (SE) based sieving mechanism [59]. The PCM membrane exhibits excellent separation behavior for oil particles based on the SE principle, as reported previously [49]. In this context, the layered structure of LDHs offers superior and selective sieving of targeted molecules through its interlayer channels and intralayer pores, restricting the passage of larger molecules and resulting in high rejection. In FS2, relatively larger oily contaminants are present, and the separation follows the SE mechanism (Fig. 8A).

In the PCMLC2 membrane, separation occurs via an adsorption-based mechanism in addition to SE, due to the adsorptive nature of LDH towards organic moieties, heavy metals, and inorganic anions (Fig. 8B). The effective contact area between targeted molecules and the adsorbent (i.e., LDH) determines the membrane's separation efficiency [60]. LDHs have a layered structure that often requires further exfoliation to increase their adsorption capacity. The incorporation of CD aids in exfoliating LDH’s stacked layers, enhancing the surface area, as confirmed by BET surface area analysis. The adsorption capacities of PCM and PCMLC2 membranes for phenol are found to be 10.6 mg/g and 123.6 mg/g, respectively (Fig. S9 and Table S1). This increased adsorption capacity can be attributed to the neutral behavior of phenol at pH 6, which facilitates the formation of strong hydrogen bonds between the hydrophilic –OH and –NH groups in the CD/NiFe–LDH composite and the –OH groups in the cellulose nanofiber. Similar behavior has been observed in studies of phenol adsorption onto β-Cyclodextrin-intercalated LDH [61] and phenol adsorption using Aliquat 336 functionalized Zn-Al LDH [62]. The PCM membrane's ability to adsorb phenols is due to the presence of cellulose nanofiber. Notably, the separation process is based on the combined effects of sieving and adsorption of targeted molecules on the membrane pores or the separating layer.

Our study demonstrates the potential combination of NiFe–LDH and CD in membranes for photocatalytic application, although use of photocatalysts in the degradation of pollutants has been studied elsewhere using newer kinds of catalyst [63,64]. Our aim was to introduce this concept in case of membrane application to degrade the rejected contaminants such that membrane fouling can be controlled. Whenasemiconductorisutilizedasaphotocatalyst, internal electric field (IEF) functions as a backstage controller to provide intrinsic power on charged particles [65] which can also be applied in membrane. In pristine LDHs, sometimes lack of IEFs may occur due to laterally symmetric structures which results in reduction of charge separation rate. This prompts the development of intralayer heterojunctions to disturb the prevailed symmetry in LDH structure. The addition of CD to the prepared photocatalyst can improve the photocatalytic activity of NiFe–LDH by creating vacancies as well as enhancing IEFs [66]. CDs can absorb visible light with a long wavelength and then relax by producing UV photons with a short wavelength. This causes photoexcitation of NiFe–LDH, also known as the up-conversion effect [67]. The designed photocatalyst can be exemplified as a Z-scheme type of heterojunction where the electrons from conduction band of CD combine with the holes of valence band of NiFe–LDH which helps in preserving the half of photogenerated charges intact for participating in redox reaction [68]. In the present work, the increased photocatalytic activity of the membrane can be understood by the inclusion of CD to the photocatalyst, which not only boosts light harvesting but also promotes charge or electron-hole pair separation. The production of active oxygen radicals (O2 −, OH, OOH, etc.) by this electron-hole pair can aid in the degradation of deposited organic contaminants from membrane surface and provide a long-life span of the membrane.

Another important property of our designed membrane is its ability to protect itself from photodegradation due to exposure of UV light. Various studies have been reported where carbon dots and layered double hydroxides have been used for protecting PET and other polymers from photodegradation.

CDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm, known for their excellent photoluminescence properties. They can be used as UV absorbers or energy transfer agents to protect PET from photodegradation. CDs have strong UV absorption capabilities. When incorporated into PET, they absorb UV radiation and convert it into less harmful visible light or heat, thereby protecting the polymer matrix from UV-induced degradation [69,70]. CDs can facilitate non-radiative energy transfer processes, where the absorbed energy is dissipated harmlessly, reducing the risk of photochemical reactions within the PET matrix. Similarly, LDHs are also known for their UV blocking capabilities. LDHs can be intercalated with UV-absorbing anions (e.g., organic UV stabilizers) [71,72]. In this study, the CD/NiFe–LDH work as a hybrid UV-protective material which can effectively block UV radiation, preventing it from penetrating and degrading the PET matrix. LDHs improve the barrier properties of PET by creating a tortuous path for the diffusion of oxygen and other reactive species. This reduces the permeability of PET to these species, thereby limiting oxidative degradation. The incorporation of LDHs can enhance the thermal stability of PET, indirectly contributing to better resistance against UV-induced thermal degradation. Therefore, the synergistic effect of CDs and NiFe LDHs can effectively protect the resultant membrane from any degradation from exposure of UV-A light.

To ensure that the polymeric layer beneath the photocatalytic layer is intact from the UV-A irradiation, we have performed the tensile strength analysis of the membrane after 2 h of UV-A irradiation and compared the data with the membrane before irradiation. The results are given in Fig. 6(J) which shows that, the ultimate stress of PCMLC2 after photoirradiation is 2.16 MPa while that of PCMLC2 before photoirradiation was 2.27 MPa. This shows that the membrane retains its mechanical strength even after long exposure to the UV-A light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-cleaning property analysis of the membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the self-cleaning behaviour of the PCMLC2 membrane under photocatalytic conditions, we conducted permeation experiments followed by UV-A light exposure in a continuous cycle, as depicted in Fig. S10 (detailed procedure provided in the SM). The PCMLC2 membrane underwent an 8-hour permeation experiment, followed by UV-A light irradiation for 1 and 2 h to assess the impact of irradiation time on the degradation of organic pollutants deposited on the membrane surface. Fig. 9 A shows the photographic image of clean PCMLC2 membrane before treatment. After 8 h of permeation with FS1, some rejected contaminants are visible on the membrane surface (Fig. 9B), which disappear upon 2 h of UV-A light exposure (Fig. 9C). SEM images of the PCMLC2 membrane surface before UV-A treatment and after 1 and 2 h of UV-A treatment are shown in Fig. S11. It was observed that the organic pollutants were degraded on the membrane surface after 1 h of UV-A irradiation (Fig. S11) as evident from the SEM images of the membrane before and after treatment. However, a more significant change was observed after 2 h of UV-A exposure, where nearly all the organic pollutants deposited on the membrane surface during the 8-hour permeation experiment were removed and all blocked pores were opened. Thus, it is recommended that 2 h of UV-A treatment is the ideal irradiation time to degrade the contaminants.

The SEM images of the PCMLC2 membranes after 8 h of permeation using FS1 (Fig. 9D1) confirm the accumulation of contaminants on the membrane surface, which were removed after 2 h of UV-A irradiation (Fig. 9D2). For FS2, the SEM images of the PCMLC2 membrane after 8 h of permeation (Fig. 9E1) and after UV-A irradiation for 2 h it was observed that the rejected contaminants were effectively degraded under 2 h of UV-A irradiation (Fig. 9E2). This was again proven by the permeation experiment of the membrane after and before UV-A treatment. Upon UV-A treatment the pores were reopened and the high flux is restored, demonstrating the membrane's self-cleaning behaviour under photocatalytic conditions. The photocatalytic degradation kinetics of contaminants were also studied using Hinshelwood model [73] and is discussed in the Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic solvent resistance of the photocatalytic membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S12A in the SM shows images of the PCMLC2 membrane after 24 h of soaking in various organic solvents viz. dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dimethyl acetamide (DMAc), hexane, acetone, petroleum ether and carbon tetrachloride. These images clearly indicate that the PCMLC2 membrane retains its structure in all tested solvents, even after extended exposure. This resilience is attributed to the inherent organic solvent-resistant properties of PET and cellulose [49]. Additionally, the chemical coating of the CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite prevents its leaching from the base membrane surface. SEM cross-sectional and surface views of the membrane after soaking in different solvents (Fig. S12(B1–B6) in the SM) reveal that the CD/NiFe–LDH structure remains almost intact on the membrane surface. Therefore, it is confirmed that the designed PCMLC2 membrane can withstand prolonged contact with organic solvent-containing wastewater without any deterioration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long term performance analysis of the membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the membrane's perseverance, the best performing membrane has been put through a permeation experiment for 30 days using both FS1 and FS2 feed solution. The long-term flux and rejection graphs of FS1 are shown in Fig. 10 (A), where a very slight flux drop is noted. On the other hand, the rejection has been enhanced with time. The accumulation of contaminants on membrane surfaces and pores over time (mostly those that are not degradable under UV-A light) can serve as an explanation why the flux value has slightly reduced. However, we have not noticed any significant decrease in the flux throughout the permeation experiment. This highly attributes to the inherent antifouling ability and photocatalytic self-cleaning ability of the membrane.

The long-term permeation analysis of FS2 is also done and flux and rejection graph obtained has been shown in Fig. 10(B). Although the flux values have been slightly decreasing over time, the slope is higher than that in case of FS1. This can be explained from the proneness of phenol present in FS2 to undergo photocatalytic degradation easily under UV-A light. After photocatalytic degradation, membrane regain high flux, which made the membrane capable of surviving for long time without significant change in flux values. In case of rejection, unlike FS1, a decreasing pattern has been obtained. However, upon photoirradiation, the recovered membrane provides high rejection than the fouled membrane. From the results of long-term permeation experiment it can also be concluded that the photocatalytic activity of the membrane remains stable for during the time period of 30 days.

Due to the use of cross flow permeation experiment, the formation of cake layer is not significant as that in dead end experiment. Thus, it became easier to remove the accumulated contaminants via photocatalytic degradation. One of the major novelties of the current study is the inherent photocatalytic property of the membrane to degrade the accumulated matter without needing any external washing treatment and to reacquire the near original performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling and antimicrobial activity of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Antifouling property of the designed membrane is studied by using crude oil–water emulsion system as feed. The results have been shown in Fig. 10(D). The flux of the permeate decreases with time. A sudden rise of flux can be observed after irradiation of UV-A radiation over the membrane PCMLC2 which is not seen in case of PCM membrane. Therefore, impregnation of CD/NiFe–LDHs over the membrane provide the membrane antifouling property which helps in the membrane to be stable in long term operation. Antifouling analysis parameters are also calculated by using Equations S8, S9, S10 and S11 given in the SM. The membrane PCMLC2 exhibits highest fouling recovery ratio of 88.08 % (Table S2 of SM). The antifouling ability of the membrane PCMLC2 is further investigated by using confocal microscopy. The confocal microscope images of the PCMLC2 membrane were taken before permeation, after permeation, and after UV-A treatment of the membrane after 8 h of continuous permeation (Fig. 10(C)). In the image of the PCMLC2 membrane before permeation, a clean surface is observed. However, after 8 h of permeation, a slight deposition of oily contaminants is noted. The amount of deposited contaminant is minimal which can be explained from the inherent antifouling properties of the membrane that obtained due to its superhydrophilic nature. Moreover, in microscopic image we have not observed any accumulation of contaminants after the membrane is subjected to photoirradiation by UV-A light for a period of 2 h.

The antimicrobial activity of membrane PCMLC2 is studied against three bacterial pathogens staphylococcus aureus (gram-positive bacteria), Bacillus subtilis (gram-positive bacteria) and Escherichia coli (gram negative). The results are shown in Fig. 10(E, F, G). The membrane PCMLC2 shows its prominent antibacterial activity for the gram-positive bacterial stains where we can observe thin clear zone in the surrounding of PCMLC2. PCMLC2 also shows some antimicrobial activity against E. coli. While there is no antimicrobial activity observed for PCM membrane. The antimicrobial activity of the designed photocatalytic membrane PCMLC2 can be ascribed due to the presence of homogeneous layer of CD/NiFe–LDH composite over the membrane surface. The source material of CD is chitosan which acquires a positive charge in mild acidic conditions due to protonation of the –NH2 groups to results –NH3 +. These positively charged CD has the ability to interact with the bacterial surface materials having negative charge. Gram negative bacteria generally have cell wall made up of peptidoglycan layer which contain teichoic acid with negative charge. Thus, chitosan is capable of hydrolysing peptidoglycans by electrostatic interactions, enabling intracellularcomponents to leak out. Conversely, chitosan can alter the permeability of the outer membrane of gram-negative bacterial species by generating an ionic kindof bond. These modifications result in a rise in internal osmotic pressure and block the entry of nutrients into the cell. Finally, lack of nutrition causes death of the bacterial cell [74]. On the other hand, LDHs due to their cationic nature are adsorbed by the negatively charged bacterial cell wall by electrostatic attraction. Large specific surface area of the CD/NiFe–LDH composite allows it to effectively enhance its adsorption behaviour on the surface of bacterial cell wall which results in the death or inhibition of the bacterial species. Moreover, during photocatalytic treatment reactive oxygen species generated from LDHs greatly influence on the inhibition of bacterial growth on surface of the membrane [75]. Therefore, impregnation of CD/NiFe–LDHs on the membrane surface also inhibits the microbial growth over the membrane surface and enable it to act as a strong barrier against bacteria that may be present in the wastewater. The inclusion of the antimicrobial property will protect the membrane from biofouling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison study of this work with reported literatures
ce:section.ce:para: The results obtained in the current study have been compared with several recent published works, as summarized in Table 2 of the main manuscript. Most of these studies focus on the separation of organic contaminants from wastewater. The present work demonstrates superior performance in terms of high flux, rejection, and durability. Organic solvent resistance and antimicrobial properties are critical criteria for wastewater treatment membranes, yet these aspects are not extensively discussed in most of the reported literature. Our study emphasizes the design of a self-cleaning, antifouling, antimicrobially active, and organic solvent-resistant membrane for industrial wastewater treatment, utilizing waste plastic as a raw material. This approach contributes to a sustainable future. In conclusion, this work presents a robust photocatalytic membrane derived from waste materials, offering superior performance in wastewater treatment compared to recent studies.

The present method of membrane design has high commercial viability and can be easily upscaled for large volume separation. Dip coating method that has been employed in our study have commercial viability for large scale production of the membrane. Moreover, the light assisted permeation experimental set up (Fig. 2) that we have developed have high commercial demand. Secondly, the synthesized membrane and materials are benign to the environment and have not possess any bad impact on the environment. Carbon dots were synthesized from bio source (chitosan). To the best of our knowledge NiFe-LDH have no environmental impact that have been reported till date. Cellulose used in the study is a biopolymer. Moreover, we have utilised waste PET bottles and tried to upcycle it which will help in mitigating environmental pollution obtained from plastic and will promote sustainability too. No secondary pollutants have been generated as a byproduct during the separation process rather the rejected contaminants have been photodegraded/mineralised to harmless products CO2 and water.

This study has successfully demonstrated a simple strategy for fabricating a multifunctional TFN membrane. By employing in situ wrapping of CDs over a NiFe–LDH structure and impregnating them onto a hydrophilic polymeric membrane derived from waste materials using glutaraldehyde as a cross-linking agent, we achieved an efficient, defect-free, homogeneous photocatalyst layer on the membrane surface. The CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite layer, combined with the mesoporosity of the base membrane, selectively rejects oily organics while facilitating the easy passage of water due to the superhydrophilicity of the hybrid system. The photocatalytic activity of the CD/NiFe–LDH nanocomposite effectively degrades oily contaminants on the membrane under UV-A irradiation, maintaining high permeability during prolonged operation. The kinetic study of phenol degradation under photo-irradiation indicated a half-life of 37.36 min. Additionally, the mineralization rate, measured via COD, confirmed the conversion of phenol to CO2 and H2O. The inherent fouling-resistant properties of the CD/NiFe–LDH, combined with the hydrophilic PET-CNF, effectively resist fouling by oily organics and the formation of a cake layer. Flux and rejection measurements identified the PCMLC2 membrane, coated twice with 0.6 % CD/NiFe–LDH, as the best performer, exhibiting maximum fluxes of approximately 42 Lm−2 h−1 for oil–water systems and 48 Lm−2 h−1 for phenol feed solutions, demonstrating its practical applicability in wastewater treatment. Additionally, the membrane exhibits antimicrobial activity, mitigating biofouling, and shows resistance to organic solvents, as well as high mechanical and thermal stability, making it suitable for treating organic-containing wastewater under harsh conditions. Our research successfully designed a high-performance, multi-strategic membrane that combines efficient separation technology with photocatalytic self-cleaning behavior, particularly useful for wastewater treatment in the petroleum and other industrial sectors. This work contributes to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) 6 (clean water and sanitation) and SDG 9 (industry, innovation, and infrastructure), and supports the advancement of a circular economy.
